signals_found.value: marine haze thinning over the Pacific

opening_excerpt:
The ranking just went out. You're number three. Your hands scan the room for who's watching you slip.

You are in the car and your mind has moved to the deal that hasn't closed yet. The term sheet is out but not signed. The hire is offered but hasn't accepted. The acquisition is progressing but isn't final. The awareness of open loops is constant. Open positions are exposure. Exposure is risk. Risk keeps you awake. This is the deal that isn't closed—the deal that exists in potential, the transaction that is not yet settled, the awareness that nothing is final until it is final and until then you are holding uncertainty.

That's the deal that isn't closed. It will come up throughout this book — not because it's the only thing happening, but because it tends to be underneath most of what we're going to look at.

The day continues. Your phone buzzes. A message from your boss. "Great job in there." You don't respond yet. You finish the coffee. The cup is empty. The world is exactly as it was. Except you did the thing. You gave the presentation. Your voice didn't fail. Your hands didn't fail. Your mind didn't fail. Venice Boulevard toward the water is still busy. marine haze thinning over the Pacific is still sharp. the Expo Line westbound still moves beneath everything. You're still standing in this coffee shop. But something has shifted. Not in the room. In you. In the knowing that you can do this. You can stand in front of people. You can speak. You can deliver. The cup in your hand is empty. You toss it. You turn toward the door.

Stay with the moment a second longer. The pattern usually shows itself before the explanation does.

Underneath the feeling is a simple mechanism: the deal that isn't closed treats every decision like a permanent threat. When the alarm runs the response, your nervous system treats uncertainty like danger and asks for impossible certainty. That is why ordinary moments feel heavy.

The point is that the deal that isn't closed treats every decision like a permanent threat.

I have noticed a form of anxiety that emerges specifically from the gap between the position you hold and the imposter awareness that you do not actually know what you are doing. This is not the same as lacking competence. You have demonstrated competence. You have the track record. The promotions and compensation confirm it. But there is a separate system running that is tracking the gap between the image of expertise and the private experience of figuring things out. Because everyone is figuring things out.
